question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getCache()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the singleton pattern of getCache() (which initializes a single Cache instance shared across all requests) to cause cache key namespace collisions between different transaction endpoints, where a crafted URL for one transaction type produces the same MD5 hash as a different transaction's URL, serving cross-transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any authentication or authorization check in responseCacheCheckHandler() before serving cached transaction data, allowing any user to query any transaction's cached response regardless of whether they are a party to that transaction, leaking sensitive transaction details?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CACHE_KEY_VERSION_SUFFIX = '-v1' appended to every cache key to predict the exact Redis key for any transaction endpoint (MD5(url) + '-v1'), enabling targeted cache invalidation or poisoning attacks against specific transaction lookups?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a very long if-none-match header value to responseCacheCheckHandler(), causing the string comparison `conditionalHeader === cachedResponse.headers[ETAG_HEADER]` to consume excessive CPU time (if ETags are long), contributing to a denial-of-service condition that delays transaction status responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the cache serves the full transaction response body (res.send(body)) without any integrity verification of the cached data, meaning that if Redis is compromised or the cache entry is corrupted, the attacker can cause the mirror node to serve falsified transaction data (e.g., showing a failed transaction as successful)?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the race condition between responseCacheCheckHandler() (which reads the cache) and responseCacheUpdateHandler() (which writes to the cache) by submitting a transaction and immediately querying its status, causing the cache to store an intermediate/partial transaction state that persists for the TTL duration, misleading other users about the transaction's finality?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit Unicode normalization differences in req.originalUrl (e.g., using different Unicode representations of the same transaction ID characters) to generate different MD5 hashes for semantically identical transaction URLs, bypassing the cache and forcing fresh database reads that may reflect different transaction states at different times?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `const headers = {...cachedResponse.headers, ...{[CACHE_CONTROL_HEADER]: \`public, max-age=${redisTtl}\`}}` spread operation to have attacker-influenced headers from a poisoned cache entry override legitimate response headers, injecting malicious cache-control directives that cause downstream clients to cache falsified transaction data indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the delete headers[contentTypeHeader] operation in the clientCached branch (when if-none-match matches) to cause a 304 response without a content-type header, confusing clients about the format of the cached transaction data and causing them to misparse the transaction body on subsequent full requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the condition `responseBody && responseCacheKey && (isUnmodified || httpStatusCodes.isSuccess(res.statusCode))` by triggering a 304 response path (isUnmodified = true) for a transaction endpoint, causing responseCacheUpdateHandler() to cache a transaction response with statusCode = httpStatusCodes.OK.code even when the actual response was 304, permanently storing an incorrect status code in Redis?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send a crafted accept-encoding header with an extremely long value to new Negotiator(req) in negotiate(), causing excessive memory allocation or CPU usage during encoding negotiation for a cached transaction response, resulting in denial of service that prevents legitimate transaction status queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the timing difference between a cache hit (served from Redis) and a cache miss (served from DB) for transaction endpoints to perform a timing side-channel attack, determining whether a specific transaction has been recently queried and is in the cache, leaking information about transaction activity patterns?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cacheKeyGenerator() hashes req.originalUrl (which includes both path and query string) without normalizing case, allowing case variations in transaction IDs (e.g., /api/v1/transactions/0X1A vs /api/v1/transactions/0x1a) to generate different cache keys for the same transaction, causing cache fragmentation and inconsistent transaction status responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `config.response.headers.default` spread in the isUnmodified branch (`{...config.response.headers.default, ...res.getHeaders(), ...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})}`) to have attacker-influenced response headers override default security headers in the cached transaction response, weakening security controls for all subsequent users querying that transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a cache-busting mechanism in responseCacheCheckHandler() to cause a victim to receive a cached transaction response showing an old transaction state (e.g., pending) even after the transaction has reached consensus, by querying the endpoint before the TTL expires and the cache is refreshed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[requestStartTime] || Date.now()` fallback in responseCacheCheckHandler() to manipulate elapsed time logging, by crafting requests that cause the requestStartTime to be absent, obscuring the actual response time and making cache-based transaction tampering harder to detect in logs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the deletion of CONTENT_ENCODING_HEADER, CONTENT_LENGTH_HEADER, and VARY_HEADER from cached headers in responseCacheUpdateHandler(), causing the cached transaction response to be served without these headers on cache hits, leading to HTTP/1.1 compliance issues where clients cannot correctly determine the length or encoding of the transaction response body?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit path traversal sequences in req.originalUrl (e.g., /api/v1/transactions/../transactions/0.0.10-123) that are not normalized before MD5 hashing in cacheKeyGenerator(), generating a different cache key than the canonical URL and bypassing a cached response that correctly shows a transaction as failed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared singleton cache (getCache()) across all users to perform a cache poisoning attack where they first trigger caching of a transaction response with specific data, then cause other users querying the same transaction to receive the poisoned cached response, falsifying the transaction's status for all users during the TTL window?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with accept-encoding: gzip to negotiate() for a transaction endpoint where cachedResponse.compressed is false, causing the function to skip the gzip branch and set only content-length via getUncompressedLength(), but if the body was modified in Redis to be compressed, the client receives garbled transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheCheckHandler() does not validate the integrity of cachedResponse.statusCode retrieved from Redis, allowing a cache entry with a manipulated statusCode (e.g., 200 instead of 400) to cause the middleware to serve a success response for a transaction that actually failed, misleading users about transaction outcomes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `ttl > 0` check in responseCacheUpdateHandler() by causing a transaction endpoint to return a cache-control header with max-age=0 (which getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault() returns as 0, skipping caching), preventing the transaction response from being cached and forcing repeated database queries that may return inconsistent transaction states during high-load periods?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the MD5 hash truncation (128-bit output) in cacheKeyGenerator() combined with the birthday paradox to find two transaction URLs that collide after approximately 2^64 attempts, causing the cache to serve one transaction's data in response to queries for a different transaction, enabling cross-transaction data leakage?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any rate limiting in responseCacheCheckHandler() to send thousands of requests per second to transaction endpoints, each with a unique URL variant that misses the cache, causing a cache stampede that overwhelms the database with transaction queries and denies service to legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `const cachedResponse = Object.assign(new CachedApiResponse(), redisValue)` pattern where redisValue is parsed from Redis JSON without schema validation, allowing a Redis entry with missing or null fields (e.g., null statusCode) to cause responseCacheCheckHandler() to serve a malformed transaction response that crashes or confuses the client?
